---
title: "![](images/icons/icon_11.png){.title-icon} Outcomes"
subtitle: "Extract outcome dates and censoring - filtered to your cohort"
date: last-modified
---

::: {.callout-warning}
**Under development.** Structural outline - to be expanded. The pattern is the same as in [Extract from LPR](09b_udtraek_fra_lpr.qmd), now filtered to your cohort.
:::

Your **outcome** is typically the date of the first event after index - e.g. a diagnosis (LPR). But to calculate follow-up time you also need the dates on which a person **stops being at risk** for the outcome: **death** (DODSAARS) and **emigration** (VNDS). You extract each date as its own table with `pnr` + date, filtered to the cohort.

<details>
<summary>Why dates other than the outcome itself?</summary>

In a **time-to-event** study you follow each person from the index date until the outcome occurs, *or* until the person can no longer have the outcome observed. A person who **dies** or **emigrates** disappears from the Danish registers, and you can no longer know whether they later developed the outcome. So you **censor** them on the death or emigration date. You extract these dates here so that in [Phase 12](12_data-manipulation.qmd) you can calculate correct follow-up time: the earliest of outcome, death, emigration and end of study.

If a competing event (e.g. death) **precludes** your outcome rather than just ending follow-up, it is a *competing risk*, which is handled in the analysis, see [Phase 13 - Analysis](13_analyse.qmd).

</details>

## The pattern

```r
# unique() removes duplicates, so you get one pnr per person - a clean list of the cohort's people
cohort_pnrs <- unique(readRDS("path/to/full_cohort.rds")$pnr)   # your cohort from Phase 10

# Example: first dementia diagnosis after index (outcome)
outcome <- open_dataset("path/to/lpr_diag/") %>%
  rename_with(tolower) %>%
  filter(pnr %in% !!cohort_pnrs) %>%          # ONLY your cohort
  # ... get the contact date from lpr_adm and filter on diagnosis codes - see Phase 9 ...
  collect() %>%
  group_by(pnr) %>% arrange(event_date) %>% slice(1) %>% ungroup()   # keep only first event per person

saveRDS(outcome, "sti/til/extract_outcome.rds")
```

`group_by(pnr) + arrange() + slice(1)` keeps only the **first** event per person: for an outcome definition you rarely need more than the first. The pattern is explained in [Long ↔ wide format](12a_langt-bredt.qmd).

::: {.callout-note}
**Two kinds of joins - don't confuse them.** Some joins assemble **one extract** - e.g. LPR's contact and diagnosis registers, joined to get `pnr` + date + diagnosis in one table (see [Extract from LPR](09b_udtraek_fra_lpr.qmd)). That is **not** the same as linking the finished extract onto your cohort; that happens later, in [Link your extracts](12b_joins.qmd), once all your extractions are done.
:::

- **Censoring:** also extract date of death from DODSAARS (`d_dodsdto`) and emigration from VNDS (`indud_kode == "U"`) - each as its own table, see [Overview of registers](15d_register_reference.qmd).
- **Save each extraction as its own `.rds` file.** An `.rds` file can hold several columns (it does for covariates, for example) - for outcomes it is typically `pnr` + one date column. In [Phase 12](12_data-manipulation.qmd) you link them all onto the cohort and calculate follow-up time from the outcome, death and emigration dates.

## See also
- [Extract from LPR](09b_udtraek_fra_lpr.qmd) - the diagnosis pattern in detail
- [Phase 12 - Assemble and prepare the dataset](12_data-manipulation.qmd) - join outcomes to the cohort
